metadata.title: J&J talc + the 'Texas two-step' — the corporate liability-engineering playbook (and the courts that keep rejecting it)
metadata.status: the litigation, the three bankruptcy attempts, and the settlement offers are FACT (court records/SEC/reporting); the third attempt's OUTCOME is reported divergently across sources (presented BOTH with dates below); the carcinogenicity is contested-in-science but plaintiffs have won verdicts; the 'liability-engineering playbook' read is the graded thesis; dignified victim framing
metadata.frame: The cleanest worked example of CORPORATE LIABILITY-ENGINEERING — and the connective node for the mass-tort cluster (opioids/PFAS/asbestos). Johnson & Johnson faces ~68,000 talc claims (ovarian cancer + mesothelioma) and tried THREE TIMES to escape them via the 'Texas two-step': use Texas's divisive-merger statute to spin the liabilities into a shell subsidiary (LTL Management -> Red River Talc), then bankrupt ONLY that subsidiary to cap/discharge all claims while the $400B+ parent stays solvent. Courts rejected it all three times (latest dismissed Mar 31 2025). This is the same liability-containment family as DuPont->Chemours (PFAS spinoff) and Purdue/Sackler (bankruptcy releases struck by SCOTUS) — so this block adds a Liability_Engineering thesis node linking them. Ties to spec-opioid-crisis (SCOTUS Purdue), spec-pfas-forever-chemicals (Chemours spinoff), the asbestos/Camp-Lejeune backlog, JNJ (a node).

the_litigation: J&J faces ~68,029 pending talcum-powder lawsuits in the federal MDL (June 2026) alleging its talc-based baby powder caused OVARIAN CANCER and MESOTHELIOMA (from asbestos contamination of talc). J&J stopped selling talc-based baby powder globally (2023, switching to cornstarch). Verdicts have gone both ways; a recent example is an $8M Massachusetts mesothelioma verdict (June 2025). The science is contested (J&J maintains its talc is asbestos-free and safe), but juries have repeatedly found for plaintiffs. grade: claims/MDL count/verdicts FACT; causation contested-in-science.
the_texas_two_step: The maneuver: under Texas's divisive-merger statute, a company splits into two — loading ALL the tort liabilities into a new shell entity while the operating business + assets go to another — then puts ONLY the liability-shell into Chapter 11. This freezes all litigation (the automatic stay) and tries to force a single bankruptcy settlement, even though the real, solvent parent never files. J&J ran it THREE times: LTL Management (Oct 2021) -> dismissed (Third Circuit, Jan 2023: not in financial distress, bad-faith filing); LTL refiled (2023) -> dismissed (2023); Red River Talc (filed Sept 2024).
the_third_attempt_conflicting_reports: The OUTCOME of the third (Red River) attempt is reported divergently — both accounts, with dates, presented rather than picking one: (A) PROPOSED-SETTLEMENT framing (2024-25): J&J reported ~83% of voting claimants supported an ~$8-10B plan and several outlets described a >$10B compensation fund as effectively created (some 2026 settlement-marketing/SEO pages, e.g. lawfold.com, still state the plan 'reached court approval'). (B) DISMISSAL framing (dated, authoritative): on 31 Mar 2025 the US Bankruptcy Court (S.D. Tex.) DISMISSED the Red River filing; contemporaneous legal reporting (Bailey Glasser, BioSpace, Reuters) said J&J would NOT appeal and would return to the tort system, and the June-2026 MDL count (~68,029 pending) is consistent with claims being back in the tort system, NOT discharged. WEIGHT: the dated court-record + March-2025 legal reporting (B) is the stronger, more-recent account; (A) appears to describe the pre-dismissal proposed plan / the creditor vote and, in the SEO pages, to be stale or erroneous. Both retained; reader can adjudicate by date. grade: filings + the Mar-2025 dismissal + the MDL count FACT; the 'approved/fund-created' framing UNSUPPORTED as of the dated record.
the_settlement_offers: J&J's offers escalated as it sought a global resolution: ~$8.9B, then a plan pitched as ~$10B for current+future claimants (nominally ~$13.7B over 25 years, ~$6.5-8B present value). All were tied to the bankruptcy vehicle the courts rejected — so the money was contingent on a maneuver that failed, and claimants are back in the tort system. grade: fact (the offer figures).
the_liability_engineering_playbook: The thesis this block crystallizes: large defendants increasingly try to CONTAIN mass-tort liability through corporate/bankruptcy ENGINEERING rather than pay in the tort system — (a) the Texas two-step / divisive merger (J&J); (b) spinning liabilities into a separate public entity (DuPont -> Chemours/Corteva for PFAS); (c) non-consensual third-party bankruptcy RELEASES to shield owners (Purdue/Sacklers). The judicial check is tightening: courts dismissed J&J's three two-steps, and the SUPREME COURT struck the Purdue releases (Harrington v. Purdue, 2024). The §524(g) asbestos trust system (the foundational template) is the legitimate, court-sanctioned version this all descends from. grade: the playbook + the judicial pushback are FACT; 'systematic strategy' is the labeled synthesis.
the_honest_reading: J&J talc is the canonical liability-engineering case: real claims (68k) + contested-but-jury-credited harm; a solvent parent repeatedly trying to offload the liability into a bankrupt shell; and courts repeatedly refusing. The settlement money was real but CONTINGENT on the rejected maneuver. The deeper finding is structural and cross-cutting (the Liability_Engineering node): the same containment playbook recurs across talc, PFAS (Chemours), and opioids (Sackler releases) — and the courts/SCOTUS are the binding constraint. Dignified framing for claimants. Overlay; excluded from the proofs.

sources.name: Bailey Glasser — Texas court sinks J&J's third bankruptcy try (Mar 2025)

Verus — J&J bankruptcy / 'Texas Two-Step' talc strategy explained
ConsumerNotice — talcum powder lawsuits: latest actions & settlements

edges.raw_instrument: ~68k MDL talc claims (ovarian cancer + mesothelioma); stopped selling talc baby powder 2023; $8M MA verdict 2025
created the shell subsidiary (LTL Management -> Red River Talc) to hold talc liabilities
the bankrupt liability-shell vehicle for the maneuver
uses Texas's divisive-merger statute to split liabilities from assets
maneuver to cap/discharge all talc claims via bankruptcy (offered ~$8-10B); failed
rejected J&J's two-step THREE times (Red River dismissed 31 Mar 2025; J&J returns to tort system)
divisive-merger + bankrupt-the-shell containment of mass-tort liability
DuPont spun PFAS liabilities into Chemours/Corteva — the spinoff variant
Purdue bankruptcy non-consensual third-party releases to shield owners — the release variant
Harrington v. Purdue (2024) struck non-consensual releases — the judicial constraint on the playbook
